title: Charmed Life - Chapter 11 (Fragment 2)
text:
Janet looked nervously at Cat. Cat sighed and felt in his pockets. All he had was half a crown. He offered it to Mr. Baslam.

Mr. Baslam backed away from it with a hurt, drooping look, like a whipped St. Bernard. “Fifty-five quid I ask for, and you offer me half a crown! Son, are you having a joke on me?”

“It’s all either of us has got,” said Cat, “at the moment. But we each get a crown piece every week. If we give you that, we’ll have paid you back in—” He did hurried calculations. Ten shillings a week, fifty-two weeks in a year, twenty-six pounds a year. “It’ll only take two years.” Two years was an appalling time to be without money. Still, Mr. Baslam had got Gwendolen her dragons’ blood, and it seemed fair that he should be paid.

But Mr. Baslam looked more hurt than ever. He turned away from Cat and Janet and gazed mournfully up at the Castle walls. “You live in a place like this, and tell me you can only get hold of ten bob a week! Don’t play cruel games with me. You can lay your hands on no end of lucre if you puts your minds to it.”

“But we can’t, honestly,” Cat protested.

“I think you should try, young gentleman,” said Mr. Baslam. “I’m not unreasonable. All I’m asking is twenty quid part payment, interest of ten percent included, and the price of the shutting-up spell thrown in. That should come quite easy to you.”

“You know perfectly well it won’t!” Janet said indignantly. “You’d better keep those earrings. Your stuffed rabbit may look pretty in them.”

Mr. Baslam gave her a very whipped look. At the same time, a thin, singing noise began to come from the palm of his hand where the earrings lay. It was too faint for Cat to pick out the words, but it put paid to any notion that Mr. Baslam had been lying. Mr. Baslam’s drooping look became less whipped. He looked more like a bloodhound hot on the trail. He let the earrings slide between his fat fingers and fall on the gravel.

“There they lie,” he said, “if you care to stoop for them. I may remind you, young lady, that trade in dragons’ blood is illicit, illegal, and banned. I’ve obliged you in it. You’ve fobbed me off. Now I’m telling you that I need twenty quid by next Wednesday. That should give you time. If I don’t get it, then Chrestomanci hears of the dragons’ blood Wednesday evening. And if he does, then I wouldn’t be in your shoes, young lady, not for twenty thousand quid and a diamond tiara. Have I made myself clear?”

He had, appallingly. “Suppose we give you the dragons’ blood back?” Cat suggested desperately. Gwendolen had taken Mr. Baslam’s dragons’ blood with her, of course, but there was always that huge jar of it in Mr. Saunders’ workshop.

“What would I do with dragons’ blood, son?” said Mr. Baslam. “I’m not a warlock. I’m only a poor supplier, and there’s no demand for dragons’ blood around here. It’s the money I need. Twenty quid of it, by next Wednesday, and don’t forget.” He gave them a bloodhound nod which flapped his eyes and his cheeks, and edged back into the rhododendrons. They heard him rustling stealthily away.

“What a nasty old man!” Janet said in a shaken whisper. “I wish I really was Gwendolen. I’d turn him into a four-headed earwig. Ugh!” She bent and scrabbled the earrings up off the gravel.

Immediately the air by the door was filled with high, singing voices. “I belong to Caroline Chant! I belong to Caroline Chant!”

vocabulary.definition: Causing shock or dismay; horrific.
vocabulary.example: Two years was an appalling time to be without money.

vocabulary.definition: To move backwards away from something or someone.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Baslam backed away from it with a hurt, drooping look.

vocabulary.definition: Officially or legally prohibit.
vocabulary.example: Trade in dragons’ blood is illicit, illegal, and banned.

vocabulary.definition: The desire of purchasers, consumers, clients etc. for a particular commodity, service or other item.
vocabulary.example: There’s no demand for dragons’ blood around here.

vocabulary.definition: In a way showing great urgency or despair.
vocabulary.example: Cat suggested desperately to give the dragons’ blood back.

vocabulary.definition: Hanging or bending downwards.
vocabulary.example: With a hurt, drooping look, like a whipped St. Bernard.

vocabulary.definition: To deceive or get rid of someone by giving excuses or false information.
vocabulary.example: You’ve fobbed me off.

vocabulary.definition: Look steadily with sadness or sorrow.
vocabulary.example: He gazed mournfully up at the Castle walls.

vocabulary.definition: To look steadily and intently, especially in admiration or thought.
vocabulary.example: He gazed mournfully up at the Castle walls.

vocabulary.definition: Forbidden by law or statute.
vocabulary.example: Trade in dragons’ blood is illicit, illegal, and banned.

vocabulary.definition: Forbidden by law, rules, or custom.
vocabulary.example: Trade in dragons’ blood is illicit, illegal, and banned.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner indicating anger or annoyance at something perceived as unfair.
vocabulary.example: Janet said indignantly, “You’d better keep those earrings.”

vocabulary.definition: Additional money paid by a borrower to a lender above the principal amount.
vocabulary.example: Interest of ten percent included.

vocabulary.definition: To obtain or find something, especially with difficulty.
vocabulary.example: You can lay your hands on no end of lucre if you puts your minds to it.

vocabulary.definition: Money or profits, especially when regarded as sordid or distasteful.
vocabulary.example: You can lay your hands on no end of lucre if you puts your minds to it.

vocabulary.definition: Bad or very unpleasant.
vocabulary.example: “What a nasty old man!” Janet said.

vocabulary.definition: A conception or belief about something.
vocabulary.example: It put paid to any notion that Mr. Baslam had been lying.

vocabulary.definition: A payment made that partially covers the total amount due.
vocabulary.example: I’m asking twenty quid part payment, interest of ten percent included.

vocabulary.definition: To express objection or disapproval.
vocabulary.example: Cat protested he couldn’t honestly get more money.

vocabulary.definition: To bring something to an end or make it impossible.
vocabulary.example: It put paid to any notion that Mr. Baslam had been lying.

vocabulary.definition: Make a soft, muffled crackling sound like that caused by the movement of dry leaves or paper.
vocabulary.example: They heard him rustling stealthily away.

vocabulary.definition: To grab or scratch about frantically to collect something.
vocabulary.example: She scrabbled the earrings up off the gravel.

vocabulary.definition: A magical spell used to silence or close something down.
vocabulary.example: The price of the shutting-up spell thrown in.

vocabulary.definition: In a secretive or sneaky way to avoid notice.
vocabulary.example: They heard him rustling stealthily away.

vocabulary.definition: Bend one's head or body forward and downward.
vocabulary.example: “There they lie,” he said, “if you care to stoop for them.”

vocabulary.definition: A person or organization that provides something needed.
vocabulary.example: I’m only a poor supplier, and there’s no demand for dragons’ blood.

vocabulary.definition: To transform someone or something into a different state or thing.
vocabulary.example: I’d turn him into a four-headed earwig.

vocabulary.definition: In a state of submission or exhaustion, often beaten or defeated.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Baslam gave her a very whipped look.
